coredata.dc:title: Aptamer-modified carbon dots for enhancement of photodynamic therapy of cancer cells

coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Menghui
Wu, Xiaoyan
Liang, Qingjing
Yu, Feng
Xu, Jiahui
Li, Zijian
Bi, Hong
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as promising materials for photodynamic therapy (PDT) because of their intriguing photochemical properties, high stability, and low toxicity. However, achieving accurate delivery of CDs based photosensitizers into tumor sites remains a grand challenge. Herein, AS1411 aptamer, a short oligonucleotide sequence with high binding specificity to tumor cells, was chemically decorated to the surface of Asp-CDs to obtain Asp-CDs@AS1411. The aptamer modified Asp-CDs@AS1411 not only retains the excellent properties of Asp-CDs such as high singlet oxygen quantum yield (up to 50%), bright photoluminescence, and good stability, but also features specific recognition and markedly enhanced phototoxicity (4 times) to tumor cells. These combined attributes make Asp-CDs@AS1411 a promising PDT agent for efficient tumor treatment. More importantly, the reported strategy paves a new avenue for developing high performance PDT materials from surface functionalized CDs featuring high photosensitivity and targeting ability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Aptamer
Photodynamic therapy
Photosensitizer
Fluorescence
Cancer cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro photodynamic therapy for tumor
Synthesis and characterization of Asp-CDs and Asp-CDs@AS1411
ROS production of Asp-CDs and Asp-CDs@AS1411

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (Grant Nos. 52172033, 51772001, 22005280 ), and the Anhui Province Key Research and Development Plan Project International Science and Technology Cooperation Special Project (No. 202004b11020015 ). We gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Key R&D Program of China (Grant No. 2021YFA1600202 ). We acknowledge the support of the Key Laboratory of Structure and Functional Regulation of Hybrid Materials of Ministry of Education, and the Key Laboratory of Functional Inorganic Material Chemistry of Anhui Province, China. We also acknowledge the support of the Key Laboratory of Environment-Friendly Polymer Materials of Anhui Province, and Anhui Key Laboratory of Modern Biomanufacturing, Anhui University, China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of Asp-CDs, the inset shows the magnified TEM image of an individual Asp-CDs; (b) the particle size distribution of Asp-CDs, the diameter of the corresponding dot distribution histogram from 100 CDs; (c) Raman spectra; (d) XPS survey spectra of Asp-CDs; (e) C1s and (f) O1s spectra of Asp-CDs.

(a) DLS of Asp-CDs and the nanoprobe Asp-CDs@AS1411; (b) Zeta potentials of Asp-CDs and the nanoprobe Asp-CDs@AS1411; (c) UV−vis absorption spectra of AS1411, Asp-CDs, Asp-CDs@AS1411; (d) PL excitation and PL emission spectra of Asp-CDs and Asp-CDs@AS1411; (e)Asp-CDs@AS1411 fluorescence intensity in the pH = 5–8 range; (f) FL intensity change of Asp-CDs@AS1411 for 35 days, inset: photograph of Asp-CDs@AS1411 solution irradiated under UV at 0 d, 10 d, and 35 d

Calculations of the 1O2 QY of Asp-CDs and Asp-CDs@AS1411: (a) UV–vis absorption spectra of the ABDA 1O2 trap in the presence of RB at different irradiation times; (b) The absorption spectrum of RB; (c) Decomposition rate constants of ABDA by RB; (d) UV–vis absorption spectra of ABDA photobleaching by Asp-CDs at different irradiation times; (e) The absorption spectrum of Asp-CDs; (f) Decomposition rate constants of ABDA by Asp-CDs; (g) UV–vis absorption spectra of ABDA photobleaching by Asp-CDs@AS1411 at different irradiation times; (h) The absorption spectrum of Asp-CDs@AS1411; (i) Decomposition rate constants of ABDA by Asp-CDs@AS1411.

CCK-8 results of different cells co-incubated with various concentrations of (a) Asp-CDs and (b) AS1411@Asp-CDs for 24 h; (c) LDH release from the HepG2 cells treated with Asp-CDs and AS1411@Asp-CDs at various concentrations; (d) Hematological evaluation and (e) photographs of RBCs treated with different concentrations of Asp-CDs and AS1411@Asp-CDs, respectively (n = 3).
CLSM HUVEC cells images of HUVEC and HepG2 cells co-incubated with 200 μg mL−1 Asp-CDs and Asp-CDs@AS1411 under 405 nm excitation, respectively. Scale bar =10 μm.

(a) Microscopy images of living HepG2 cells treated with Asp-CDs and Asp-CDs@AS1411solutions for 4 h and labeled with calcein-AM and of dead cells labeled with PI before and after LED irradiation, respectively; Viability of (b) HepG2 and (c) Hela cells incubated with different concentrations of Asp-CDs and Asp-CDs@AS1411 under dark and irradiated conditions by LED (400–500 nm, 15.0 mW cm–2) for 10 min, respectively (n = 3). Scale bar = 100 μm.
Schematic illustration of the preparation of Asp-CDs@AS1411.

head.ce:title: Aptamer-modified carbon dots for enhancement of photodynamic therapy of cancer cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University, Hefei, 230601, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University, Hefei, 230601, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Anhui University, Hefei, 230601, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bSchool of Materials Science and Engineering, Anhui University, Hefei, 230601, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as promising materials for photodynamic therapy (PDT) because of their intriguing photochemical properties, high stability, and low toxicity. However, achieving accurate delivery of CDs based photosensitizers into tumor sites remains a grand challenge. Herein, AS1411 aptamer, a short oligonucleotide sequence with high binding specificity to tumor cells, was chemically decorated to the surface of Asp-CDs to obtain Asp-CDs@AS1411. The aptamer modified Asp-CDs@AS1411 not only retains the excellent properties of Asp-CDs such as high singlet oxygen quantum yield (up to 50%), bright photoluminescence, and good stability, but also features specific recognition and markedly enhanced phototoxicity (4 times) to tumor cells. These combined attributes make Asp-CDs@AS1411 a promising PDT agent for efficient tumor treatment. More importantly, the reported strategy paves a new avenue for developing high performance PDT materials from surface functionalized CDs featuring high photosensitivity and targeting ability.

The aptamer modified Asp-CDs (Asp-CDs@AS1411) features specific recognition to cancer cells rendered by AS1411 aptamer and endow Asp-CDs@AS1411 with markedly enhanced phototoxicity.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is a major public health problem, causing nearly 10 million deaths worldwide each year [1]. Currently, the clinical cancer treatment is mainly chemotherapy, radiotherapy, and surgery, but it usually leads to various side effects, including drug resistance, immune system imbalance, or postoperative wound infection, which greatly affects the patient's cure and recovery [2,3]. Therefore, efforts have recently been made to develop new methods with high efficacy and low side effects [4]. Among them, photodynamic therapy (PDT) is becoming a promising cancer treatment method because of its non-invasive, low toxicity, no drug resistance, and few side effects [5]. The mechanism of PDT involves a photosensitizer (PS) that absorbs light with a specific wavelength, leading to the formation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) that kill cancer cells through necrosis, and apoptosis, or autophagy. Thus far, some traditional photosensitizers such as organic small molecules and semiconductor nanomaterials have disadvantages like low water solubility, insufficient photostability, and high toxicity, which limits their clinical application [6]. Therefore, the development of novel PS is urgent for phototherapy of cancer [1,5,7].

Carbon dots (CDs) is a new kind of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial with attractive physical and chemical properties such as adjustable fluorescence, facile surface modification, good water solubility, excellent photostability, and good biocompatibility [8]. They are widely used as optical functional materials in various fields such as fluorescence sensing, biological imaging, and drug delivery [9,10]. Recently, some CDs have been found to capable of harness light energy to generate ROS under photoexcitation, enabling them as effective photosensitizer to be applied in PDT of cancer [11–13]. However, most of the CDs have disadvantages of low tumor uptake due to the lack of selectivity to cancer cells [14–16]. In this regard, how to improve the tumor-targeting ability remains a grand challenge that needs to be solved.

Aptamer is a short oligonucleotide sequence which can selectively bind to small molecules or proteins with high affinity and strong specificity [17–19]. Due to the merits such as chemical stability, easy synthesis, and no immunogenicity, numerous aptamers with different sequences have been developed for the specific uses [20–22]. Among them, AS1411 aptamer is a guanine-rich oligonucleotide that can specifically recognize many types of tumor cells. Therefore, modification of AS1411 aptamer to nanocarriers such as CDs represents an effective strategy for improving their targeting specificity for tumor cells [18,23,24]. Recently, AS1411 aptamer has been modified to CDs for the specific imaging of cancer cells and imaging-guided photothermal therapy. [25,26] However, achieving targeting specificity and high PDT efficiency simultaneously in aptamer-CDs systems for tumor therapy has not been reported.

In this study, carbon dots were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method (Asp-CDs). On this basis, AS1411 aptamer was successfully modified on the surface of Asp-CDs (Asp-CDs@AS1411), giving the following advantages. For one, Asp-CDs@AS1411 retains the excellent properties of Asp-CDs such as with excellent singlet oxygen generation efficiency, good stability and high emission efficiency. For another, the surface engineering of AS1411 endows Asp-CDs with specific targeting capability to tumor cells. As a result, Asp-CDs@AS1411 features high tumor uptake and markedly enhanced phototoxicity to tumor cells. These combined attributes make Asp-CDs@AS1411 an excellent imaging nanoprobe and a promising PDT agent for efficient tumor treatment.

All chemicals and biological reagents used in this work were obtained from commercial suppliers and without further purification. Aspartic acid (98%), ethylenediamine, and commercial ethanol (99.7%) were obtained from local supplies and used as received. Ultra-pure water was used in all experiments from the Millipore system. The nucleic acid aptamer AS1411 with the sequence 5ʹ-NH2-GGTGGTGGTGGTTGTGGTGGT GGTGG-3ʹ was purchased from Shanghai Sangon Biological Engineering Technology & Services Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethyl aminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, China.

Asp-CDs were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method with aspartic acid and ethylenediamine as raw materials. Typically, 1.8 g aspartic acid and 2 mL ethylenediamine are added to ultrapure water, sealed in a poly Teflon-lined autoclave was heated at 200 °C for 6 h. The yellow samples were then collected after cooling down to room temperature. Subsequently, the collected Asp-CDs suspension was dialyzed in ultra-pure water in a 500 Da dialysis bag for 96 h, and the collected suspension was freeze-dried to obtain Asp-CDs as a brown powder.

In this study, the AS1411 aptamer grafting on the Asp-CDs was achieved by the EDC/NHS activation. Briefly, previously synthesized Asp-CDs (300 μg) were suspended in 1 mL of PBS. Then, EDC (2 mg) and NHS (1 mg) were added to the above suspension and reacted for 20 min at room temperature incubation for activation. Afterward, AS1411 aptamer solution (300 μL, 10 μM) was further added and the coupling reaction was performed at room temperature for 12 h. Subsequently, the solution after the reaction was purified by 10 kDa cutoff filters under 10,000 g for 20 min and repeated 4 times. Finally, the product was stored in PBS for further use.

The sample's morphology and structure were examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was used to measure the hydrodynamic diameters of samples. A fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-4500, Hitachi) and a UV-1800 PC spectrophotometer were used to record the sample's photoluminescence (PL) spectrum and ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorbance, respectively. A calibrated integrating sphere using a HORIBA FLSP920 system was used to assess the absolute optical quantum yield (QY). A NEXUS-870 spectrometer was used to collect Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded on a commercial spectrophotometer (ESCALAB 250). NMR spectrum was measured on a Bruker AVANCEII 400 MHz NMR spectrometer. Zeta potential analysis was performed on a Zeta size analyzer (Nano ZS90, Malvern Instruments Ltd.). At room temperature, the singlet oxygen (1O2) was assessed by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy. Fluorescent images were recorded by a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Leica SP8 DIVE).

The cell lines used in this paper are HUVEC (human umbilical vein endothelial cells), HepG2 (human hepatocellular carcinoma), SY5Y (human neuroblastoma cells), and HeLa (human cervical cancer). The above cell lines were respectively cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) and (Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium/Nutrient Mixture F-12) DMEM/F-12 medium containing 10% FBS and 100 U mL−1 penicillin-streptomycin. The cells were incubated in 5% CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C.

The biocompatibility of Asp-CDs and Asp-CDs@AS1411 was assessed on HUVEC, HepG-2, SY5Y, and HeLa cells by standard Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) assay [27,28]. Firstly, the cells were inoculated in 96-well plate and incubated with different concentrations of Asp-CDs and Asp-CDs@AS1411 (0, 0.05, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.25, 0.30 mg mL−1) for 24 h. Then, the cells were carried out standard CCK-8 assay to evaluate the surviving cells in each cell by measuring the absorbance at 450 nm with a microplate reader. The cell viability of the cells could be calculated from the percentage of the surviving cells in each incubated well compared with the control group as the mean values of triplicate parallel tests.

The fluorescence imaging potential of prepared Asp-CDs@AS1411 was firstly investigated on HUVEC, HepG2, SY5Y and HeLa cancer cells. Briefly, HUVEC, HepG2 and HeLa cells (3 × 105 cells/well) were cultured on the coverslips in a 6-well plate and incubated for 24 h. Then, various formulations of Asp-CDs and Asp-CDs@AS1411 (0.2 mg mL−1) were added to each well and co-incubated with the cells for 2 h to allow cellular uptake. Thereafter, the cells were washed carefully with PBS buffer several times and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde aqueous solution. Afterward, the cells were further washed thrice and mounted with a medium containing 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI). Finally, the samples were observed by a confocal laser scanning microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro photodynamic therapy for tumor

ce:section.ce:para: To observe the photothermal antitumor effect, live/dead cell staining was firstly conducted upon (Light Emitting Diode) LED irradiation. Briefly, HepG2 cells were cultured on a 6-well plate and co-incubated with Asp-CDs and Asp-CDs@AS1411 for 4 h to allow uptake of cells. Afterward, cells were washed carefully with PBS buffer and subsequently irradiated by LED (400–500 nm, 15 mW·cm−2) for 10 min. Thereafter, the cells were stained with calcein-AM and PI and imaged by fluorescence microscopy.

The photodynamic therapy efficacy of the tumor cells was further quantitively evaluated by CCK-8 assay. HepG2 cells were seeded in a 96-well plate at 1 × 104 cells/well and incubated in 5% CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C for 24 h. Then, HepG2 cells were incubated with PBS, Asp- CDs and Asp-CDs@AS1411 at different concentrations (PBS, 0.05, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.25, and 0.30 mg mL−1). After 24 h incubation, cells were irradiated with LED (400–500 nm, 15 mW·cm−2) for 10 min. Upon the LED (400–500 nm, 15 mW·cm−2) irradiation, cells were further incubated for 24 h and then analyzed by standard CCK-8 assay to evaluate the surviving cells in each well. The cell viability could be calculated from the absorbance at 450 nm by comparing it with the control group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of Asp-CDs and Asp-CDs@AS1411
ce:section.ce:para: Asp-CDs were synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal method with aspartic acid and ethylenediamine as the precursors (Scheme 1) [29–31]. The Asp-CDs exhibited well-dispersed and homogeneous particles with average size of 2.1 ± 0.3 nm quantified by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image ( Fig. 1a) and the size distribution results (Fig. 1b). The high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1a, inset) showed that the Asp-CDs consisted of amorphous carbon species and crystalline core structures. The lattice spacing value was 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) planes of graphite carbon. The coexistence of amorphous and crystalline carbon structures was further validated by Raman spectra, where the D band (disordered domains) and G band (graphite domains) were found at 1368 and 1610 cm−1, respectively. The intensity ratio of the D to G was determined to be 0.80 (Fig. 1c), indicating the highly graphitic structure in Asp-CDs. In addition, X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) exhibited a broad peak at 22.5° (Figure S1, Supporting Information), which is attributed to the interlayer spacing (002) of 0.39 nm. This was slightly larger than the commonly observed value (∼0.35 nm) in typical graphitic structure, which might be caused by the steric hindrance effect.

The chemical composition was then evaluated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The full XPS spectra presented in Fig. 1d shows three typical peaks: C 1 s (285 eV), N 1 s (400 eV), and O 1 s (531 eV). It suggested that Asp-CDs consisted of C, N, and O elements and the atomic ratios were calculated to 67.02%, 10.83%, and 22.15%, respectively. More importantly, the high-resolution C1s and O1s XPS spectra (Fig. 1e, 1f) revealed the presence of CO and C = O signals, which could be assigned to the carboxylic (-COOH) groups on the surface of Asp-CDs. This enables us to surface engineering of Asp-CDs by simple amidation reaction. In addition, the N 1 s XPS spectra can be fitted into four peaks (Figure S2) corresponding to 398.8 (eV), 400.1 (eV), 400.9 (eV) and 401.8 (eV), respectively. As well documented, the introduction of nitrogen-containing domains could enrich the photophysical properties of CDs, which is beneficial for the emission and ROS generation efficiency. 1H NMR spectroscopy was further employed to assess the hydrophilicity of Asp-CDs. The Hydrophilic–Lipophilic Balance (HLB) value was calculated to be 15.12, which shows good hydrophilicity of the Asp-CDs (Figure S3) [32].

To improve the targeting ability, AS1411 aptamers were chemically modified on the surface of Asp-CDs. The TEM image (Figure S4a) and corresponding size distribution (Figure S4b) show that Asp-CDs@AS1411 possess average sizes of 2.4 ± 0.4 nm similar to that of Asp-CDs (2.1 ± 0.3 nm). It implicated that the flexible segments of aptamer were difficult to be identified in TEM. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) image of Asp-CDs@AS1411 estimated the average height was 1.8 ± 0.3 nm (Figures S5a-5c), larger than that of Asp-CDs (1.5 ± 0.2 nm, Figures S5d-5f). This trend parallels that in Dynamic light scattering (DLS), where the hydrodynamic diameter increases from Asp-CDs (8.7 nm) to Asp-CDs@AS1411 (20.6 nm) ( Fig. 2a). Besides, owing to the abundance of –COOH on the surface, the zeta potentials of Asp-CDs were measured to be −12 mV. This value dropped to −22 mV for Asp-CDs@AS1411 due to the large consumption of carboxyl groups in the negatively charged AS1411 aptamer grafting process (Fig. 2b) [33]. Additional evidence for the aptamer modification was obtained from the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra and agarose gel electrophoresis (Figures S6, S7). According to FT-IR spectra of Asp-CDs@AS1411, the peak at 1040 cm−1 was attributed to the O-P stretching vibration of phosphate groups. [21,25] Meanwhile, peaks at 1650 cm−1 and 1560 cm−1 assignable to carbonyl (C = O) and amine (N–H) groups were found in the spectra of Asp-CDs@AS1411, indicating the formation of –CONH bond. Moreover, the migration resistance of Asp-CDs@AS1411 in the agarose gel gap increases and the migration speed decreases as compared with Asp-CDs. These results validated the successful decoration of aptamer on the surface of Asp-CDs.

The optical properties were then investigated. As presented in Fig. 2c, the absorption spectra of Asp-CDs have a broad excitonic absorption peak centered at 345 nm. This band remains unchanged after aptamer modification, suggesting the good stability of Asp-CDs. Instead, a shoulder peak around 260 nm, which matches well with the absorption of AS1411, emerged for Asp-CDs@AS1411, which should be derived from the surface grafted AS1411. Moreover, the introduction of AS1411 did not significantly change the fluorescence properties of Asp-CDs, as exhibited by their comparable emission quantum yields (11.68% versus 8.59%) (Figure S8), lifetimes (4.61 ns versus 3.36 ns) (Figure S9) and wavelength maxima (465 nm versus 461 nm) (Fig. 2d). Interestingly, carefully comparing the excitation spectra of Asp-CDs and Asp-CDs@AS1411 reveals that the absorption of AS1411 also contributes to the emission of Asp-CDs@AS1411. This suggested the close contact between AS1411 and the emission center of Asp-CDs, which led to an energy transfer from the former to latter. Furthermore, the aqueous solution of Asp-CDs@AS1411 was incubated under ambient conditions at pH = 5.0∼8.0 for 30 days (Figs. 2e, 2f). The negligible spectroscopic changes demonstrated the excellent stability of Asp-CDs@AS1411. In addition, the standard curve was employed to estimate the loading amount of AS1411 on Asp-CDs, which was determined to be ∼ 4.86 nmol mg−1 (Figure S10) [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: ROS production of Asp-CDs and Asp-CDs@AS1411
ce:section.ce:para: As a next step, the visible-light photosensitization behaviors were examined. In principle, PDT involvesthe utilization of three basic components: light, molecular oxygen (O2), and a PS to generate highly cytotoxic ROS to kill cancer cells through either type-I or type-II photochemical reaction mechanism. Type-I photosensitizer transfers electron to the surrounding biological molecules, yielding free radicals. These radicals can interact with oxygen and water molecules to produce hydroxyl and superoxide anions. In the type-II mechanism,the excited triplet state PS directly sensitizes O2 (the ground state is a triplet) and generates highly cytotoxic 1O2 inside cancer cells. Generally, cytotoxic 1O2 has been recognized as one of the ROS responsive for PDT [35–37]. In detail, upon photoexcitation (λ ex = 590 nm) of Asp-CDs and Asp-CDs@AS1411 with 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine as the trapping agents, strong signal was observed in the EPR spectrum (Figure S11). The signal features three lines with equal intensities, indicating the production of 1O2. This demonstrates type-II photosensitization mechanism of Asp-CDs and Asp-CDs@AS1411, which can kill cancer cells through multifarious pathways, such as induction of cell apoptosis or necrosis, stimulation of robust inflammatory immune response, and disruption of tumor microvasculature. To evaluate the 1O2 generation efficiency, 9,10-Anthracenediyl-bis(methylene)dimalonic Acid (ABDA) and rose bengal were employed as the probe and standard, respectively. Monitoring the irradiation time-dependent absorbance of ABDA could quantify the 1O2 production capability [38]. Specifically, the 1O2 quantum yield of Asp-CDs and Asp-CDs@AS1411 were up to 51.39% and 57.37% ( Figs. 3a-i), which is among the most efficient CDs-based photosensitizers (0.27∼1.3) [39,40].

Prior to PDT studies, the cytotoxicity of Asp-CDs and Asp-CDs@AS1411 was evaluated. CCK-8, Layered Double Hydroxide (LDH), and hemolysis assays were used to assess the in vitro biocompatibility. As shown in Figs 4a-b, CCK-8 results show that both cancer cells (HepG2, Hela and SY5Y) and normal cells (HUVEC) viabilities were over 80% after 24 h incubation with Asp-CDs and Asp-CDs@AS1411 at high concentrations up to 300 µg mL−1. Previous studies have revealed that the LDH concentration is directly proportional to the total number of damaged cell membranes. As shown in Fig. 4c, HepG2 cells exposed to Asp-CDs@AS1411 for 24 h lead to a slightly higher LDH leakage than those exposed to Asp-CDs under the same conditions. These results are consistent with the CCK-8 assay [41]. The hemolysis assay was further used to investigate the compatibility of Asp-CDs and Asp-CDs@AS1411 with blood (Figs. 4d, 4e). The Asp-CDs and Asp-CDs@AS1411 show mild hemolysis at 200 μg mL–1. Overall, Asp-CDs and Asp-CDs@AS1411 have relatively low toxicity to red blood cells (RBCs), allowing further in vivo applications.

To investigate the cancer-targeting specificity, HepG2, Hela, SY5Y, and HUVEC were co-incubated with Asp-CDs or Asp-CDs@AS1411 (200 μg mL–1) at 37 °C for 2.5 h. The fluorescence imaging behavior was recorded from confocal laser scanning microscope (CLMS) (Fig. 5 and Figure S12). As expected, the fluorescence signal are very weak for Asp-CDs incubated cells, and no appreciable difference was detected between the normal cells and cancer cells. In stark contrast, bright fluorescence was clearly observed for Asp-CDs@AS1411 incubated cancer cells. The high signal-to-noise ratio of fluorescence, which is distinct from its normal cell group, was attributed to the high cellular uptake of Asp-CDs@AS1411 in cancer cells. This dramatic difference unambiguously demonstrates the specific recognition between the Asp-CDs@AS1411 and cancer cells [19].

On account of the above mentioned attributes, we further explored the application of Asp-CDs@AS1411 in PDT. The non-fluorescent 2′,7′-Dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) was selected to evaluate the intracellular ROS production potential as it could be rapidly oxidized by ROS to form green-color emissive dichlorofluorescein (DCF) [42,43]. As shown in Figure S13, an intense green fluorescence of DCF is observed after co-incubating Asp-CDs@AS1411 with HepG2 cells for 4 h under a visible LED laser (400–500 nm, 15.0 mW cm–2) irradiation. The results reveal its potential as an efficient PS agent for PDT application [44,45]. To further determine the in vitro tumor cell killing effect, calcein-AM-stained cells were performed. First, both Asp-CDs and Asp-CDs@AS1411 exhibited negligible toxicity under dark conditions. Under light irradiation, the tumor cells incubated with Asp-CDs showed vivid green fluorescence, indicating no significant therapeutic effect. In stark contrast, the green fluorescence gradually faded and strong red fluorescence was observed after Asp-CDs@AS1411 incubated cancer cell exposed to 400–500 nm light. It clearly demonstrates that Asp-CDs@AS1411 feature much higher phototoxicity to cause cell apoptosis (
Fig. 6a). This was also reflected by the relative cell viability test. Specifically, the viability of HepG2, Hela SY5Y and HUVEC cells incubated with 200 μg mL–1 of Asp-CDs@AS1411 were determined to be 43%, 41%, 57% and 85% upon light irradiation for 10 min, which was notably lower than that with Asp-CDs (81%, 75%, 77% and 81%) under the same conditions (Fig. 6b, 6c 6c and Figures S14). The differences between the viability of tumor cell and normal cell (tumor cell: 41%∼57%, normal cell: 85%), together with the lowered viability of tumor cells when comparing with Asp-CDs (Asp-CDs@AS1411: 41%∼57%, Asp-CDs: 75%∼81%), should be attributed to the specific recognition of Asp-CDs@AS1411, which promotes PDT effects towards tumor cells.

For the phenotypic analysis of the antitumor activity of Asp-CDs@AS1411, the SY5Y 3D multicellular spheroids (3D MC) model, a typical model which mimics the tumor microenvironment, is established using SY5Y cells (Figure S15). As expected, the size of the 3D MCs increases rapidly over time in the absence of Asp-CDs@AS1411, indicative of no antitumor activity by merely using Asp-CDs and/or light. The antitumor efficiency is further improved when Asp-CDs@AS1411 and light is applied, as evidenced by the accelerated diminishing process of 3D MCs. After 7 days of incubation, the 3D MCs with 10 min/day of light irradiation is almost eliminated. These phenomena reveal the synergistic effect of Asp-CDs@AS1411 and light on tumor cells ablation.

In summary, a novel tumor-targeting photosensitizer Asp-CDs@AS1411 was successfully developed to promote the PDT effect of tumor cells. The synthesized Asp-CDs@AS1411 not only exhibits good water solubility, excellent biocompatibility, and high stability, but also retains the high emission efficiency and singlet oxygen quantum yield of Asp-CDs. Moreover, the targeting ability rendered by AS1411 greatly enhances the accumulation of Asp-CDs@AS1411 in tumor cells as exhibited by imaging experiment. As a result, the phototoxicity of Asp-CD@AS1411 to tumor cells was significantly higher than that of Asp-CDs. Therefore, the elegant combination of CDs with high ROS generation efficiency and aptamers with good targeting ability represents a promising strategy for developing high performance PDT materials.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (Grant Nos. 52172033, 51772001, 22005280), and the Anhui Province Key Research and Development Plan Project International Science and Technology Cooperation Special Project (No. 202004b11020015). We gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Key R&D Program of China (Grant No. 2021YFA1600202). We acknowledge the support of the Key Laboratory of Structure and Functional Regulation of Hybrid Materials of Ministry of Education, and the Key Laboratory of Functional Inorganic Material Chemistry of Anhui Province, China. We also acknowledge the support of the Key Laboratory of Environment-Friendly Polymer Materials of Anhui Province, and Anhui Key Laboratory of Modern Biomanufacturing, Anhui University, China.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.talo.2022.100161.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Hong Bi, a full professor and doctoral supervisor of the School of Materials Science and Engineering, Anhui University. Main research fields: nano-biomaterials, carbon dot-based luminescent materials and energy storage materials. So far, she has published 120 academic papers indexed by SCI, which have been cited about 3000 times, obtained 20 Chinese invention patents, and served as a reviewer and guest editor for various international academic journals.
